s1_b1: Sami wants a moon to circle his planet. Just once, clean, and come back around.
s1_b2: One goal. One full lap. Not into the planet. Not off into the dark.
s1_b3: He releases it gently. It falls straight in and hits the planet.
s1_b4: So he throws it harder. Now it tears away in a straight line and never comes back.
s1_b5: He eases off. It curves, hope rises — then sags inward and crashes anyway.
s1_b6: Faster, slower, faster. Sometimes an egg shape, sometimes a crash. He's clicking, not choosing.
s1_b7: He wasn't short of tries. He was short of the balance underneath — fall against fling.
s1_b8: The planet always pulls the moon straight toward its center. That pull never rests.
s1_b9: And the moon, left alone, only wants to go straight. An orbit is those two, matched.
s1_b10: Too slow, the pull wins — it falls in. Too fast, the straight line wins — it escapes. The lap lives in between.
s1_b11: It is the same thing as throwing a ball so fast the ground keeps curving away beneath it.
s1_b12: Before he clicks again — he builds it where he can see the pull and the path.
s1_b13: Gravity and Orbits. He sets the masses and the distance. Turns the force arrow and the path trace on.
s1_b14: Here is the real job. Not pressing play. Predicting — before he does.
s1_b15: What launch speed makes the pull and the straight line match — so the trace closes into a circle, not a spiral, not an escape?
s1_b16: Then he presses play. And he watches the trace, not the moon.
s1_b17: After — he explains the gap. Spiraled in? Launch was slow. Flew off? Too fast. The trace names his error.
s1_b18: He adjusts the speed. Predicts again. Three runs. That's all he gets.
s1_b19: His first prediction is wrong. That is not the problem. That is the point. Each miss narrows the speed.
s1_b20: The closed circle was never the homework. This is: in one sentence, your own words — why does staying in orbit mean falling forever without landing?
s1_b21: And if this felt familiar — it should. Do you remember the two rocks that pulled each other across empty space, with nothing in between?
s1_b22: And the Earth that changed the space around it — arrows crowding inward, everywhere, whether anything was there to feel them or not?
s1_b23: And the ball thrown so fast the ground curved away beneath it — falling, and never landing?
s1_b24: This orbit was all three at once. That is the concept. Now you know which videos to go back to.
